content:
2 min read
Just now

--

Between setting up Python environments for my AI-ML class and managing Git repositories for my portfolio, I spend a huge amount of time staring at the terminal. And honestly, remembering complex terminal commands is one of the most frustrating parts of development.
I constantly find myself opening a browser to Google things like, “How do I undo my last Git commit without losing the files?” or “What is the bash command to find and kill a port?”
Today, I took Copilot out of my code editor and started using GitHub Copilot in the CLI (Command Line Interface).
Instead of leaving my terminal to search for a command, I can now just ask Copilot right where I am working. I wanted to safely undo a Git commit today, so instead of guessing and accidentally deleting my work, I just typed a quick question right in the command line:
?? undo my last git commit but keep the changes in my staging area
Copilot instantly returned git reset --soft HEAD~1. It even gave me a plain-English explanation of exactly what that command would do before I ran it, so I knew it was safe to execute.
It also has a cool feature to explain errors. If a command fails and spits out a wall of red text, you can just ask it to explain the error, and it breaks it down for you.
If you are someone who gets intimidated by the command line or constantly forgets syntax, getting Copilot in your terminal is a massive time saver.
